title: Blood Cultures — Taking
oneLiner: Aseptic blood sampling (2-4 sets) from peripheral venipuncture before antibiotics in suspected sepsis — key to de-escalation and targeted therapy.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock with delayed antibiotics:** The single finding is hypotension (MAP <65 mmHg) + lactate ≥4 mmol/L. Cultures can wait 2–3 minutes for adequate preparation; start empiric antibiotics *immediately* after or even simultaneously. Do not delay antibiotics >60 minutes to obtain cultures.
- **Meningococcaemia with rapid purpura fulminans:** The finding is non-blanching petechiae/purpura that appear or spread within minutes to hours, plus fever. Cultures from two sites (blood and CSF if safe) are urgent but antibiotics should not be delayed for a lumbar puncture. Call senior and ICU early.
sections.Presentation:
- Suspected infection: fever (>38°C), rigors, hypothermia (<36°C), tachycardia, tachypnoea, altered mental state, new hypotension.
- Localising signs may be absent (especially in elderly, immunocompromised).
- Indications: any patient with suspected bacteraemia including: sepsis, meningitis, infective endocarditis, pyrexia of unknown origin, central line infection, suspected osteomyelitis/septic arthritis, pneumonia requiring admission, and all patients with suspected serious infection before antibiotics.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Contaminant vs true pathogen:** The single best differentiator is *number of positive bottles* — ≥2 positive bottles (different sites) makes true pathogen likely; 1 of 2 sets positive (especially coagulase-negative staph) suggests contamination.
- **Bacteraemia vs viral or non-infectious fever:** Differentiate by procalcitonin (PCT) if available (PCT >0.5 ng/mL favours bacterial), but do not delay cultures waiting for it.
- **Endovascular infection (endocarditis, suppurative thrombophlebitis):** Look for persistent bacteraemia (≥2 sets positive over 12–24h), new murmur, embolic phenomena. Echocardiography is key.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Thorough hand hygiene, skin disinfection (2% chlorhexidine in 70% alcohol or alcohol-based povidone-iodine), allow to dry. Palpate vein *after* disinfection (not before). Collect aerobic + anaerobic bottles (or specific order per local protocol). Volume is key — draw 8–10 mL per bottle (less in children). Use sterile technique — do not touch the puncture site after disinfection. Change gloves if you accidentally contaminate.
- **Bloods:** Full blood count, CRP, PCT, lactate, coagulation profile, renal/liver function. Additional: consider cultures from other sites (urine, sputum, swabs, CSF if indicated).
- **Imaging:** Not directly part of cultures but guided by clinical focus (chest X-ray, CT abdomen, echocardiography).
- **Special tests:** If suspect infective endocarditis — obtain 3 sets of blood cultures (ideally over 24h) before antibiotics, from different venipunctures (not from line unless suspect line infection). For central line infection — paired cultures (peripheral + central) with differential time to positivity (central grows >2h earlier suggests line source).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** qSOFA (≥2 of: RR≥22, SBP≤100, altered mentation) or NEWS2 (≥5 triggers escalation). For endocarditis: Duke criteria (major criteria: 2 positive blood cultures typical organisms, echocardiographic vegetation).
- **Never-miss test:** **Ensure adequate volume** — most common error is underfilling bottles. Target 8–10 mL per bottle (adults). Also, **re-label if drawn from a line** — mark site clearly (e.g. "peripheral", "central line lumen A").
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - If not already done: insert IV/IO access (separate site for cultures).
    - Clean skin with 2% chlorhexidine in 70% alcohol swab — use a single-use applicator, allow 30 seconds to dry.
    - Do not repalpate after disinfection; use sterile gloves.
    - Obtaining cultures: fill aerobic bottle first (if using butterfly or straight draw) or follow local order. Invert gently after filling.
    - If blood is difficult: use smaller volumes rather than no culture — but note that yield drops significantly below 5 mL per bottle.
    - Document exact time and site of each culture in notes.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - Review culture results at 24–48h. If intermediate organisms appear (e.g. coagulase-negative staph) — discuss with microbiology to decide significance.
    - Ensure subcultures and sensitivities are requested if any growth.
    - Once organism identified, tailor antibiotics from empiric to targeted (de-escalate if possible).
    - Check for need for repeat cultures (e.g. persistent fever after 48h or to confirm clearance in endocarditis/line infections).
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Not applicable for the procedure itself; follow-up cultures as per infection focus.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A — procedure is single episode, not ongoing therapy. But if patient admitted with sepsis, set observation frequency (e.g. q1h for first 4 hours, then q2–4h), fluids as per local sepsis protocol (e.g. 30 mL/kg crystalloid in first 3h), VTE prophylaxis as per usual.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- No drugs for the procedure. But crucial: **Do not give antibiotics until after cultures are drawn unless delay would be unsafe** (septic shock). If given before, document time and ideally draw cultures within 10–15 minutes of the antibiotic (still useful).
- If central line suspected: consider empiric antibiotics per local guideline (e.g., vancomycin + antipseudomonal beta-lactam). Do not list doses here — [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Delaying antibiotics for >60 minutes while struggling to get cultures. **Get the cultures first, but only if it takes <5 minutes; otherwise skip and start antibiotics.** The error is prioritising the perfect culture over timely therapy.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - Contamination — leading to false-positive cultures (coagulase-negative staph, diphtheroids, Bacillus spp.) → unnecessary antibiotics, prolonged stay.
    - Needlestick injury to operator.
    - Haematoma, nerve injury (rare).
    - Iatrogenic anaemia (if frequent large-volume draws) — uncommon.
- **Chronic:** None direct from procedure.
sections.Prognosis: - Proper culture technique directly improves antibiotic stewardship — reduces mortality by enabling de-escalation. Contamination rates should be <3% (benchmark). Each false-positive culture can add 1–2 extra hospital days and costs.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO use a sterile technique checklist:** Hand hygiene, skin prep (chlorhexidine alcohol, allow to dry), sterile gloves, no repalpation, fill to mark, invert gently. Many hospitals have a bedside checklist — use it.
- **DO take two separate sets (from two different venipunctures) before antibiotics in all stable patients with suspected sepsis.** One set is insufficient — it may miss intermittent bacteraemia.
- **DO document the site, time, and any technical difficulties** — this helps microbiology interpret results (e.g., if only 1 set positive and it was a difficult draw from a small vein, contamination more likely).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **DO NOT use alcohol swabs that have dried out** — they have reduced efficacy. Use a new one.
- **DO NOT take blood cultures from a line** unless you suspect line infection (and then do paired sets per protocol). If you do, label it clearly. Routinely using line draws increases contamination.
- **DO NOT underfill or overfill bottles** — underfill reduces yield, overfill may hemolyse or activate anticoagulant. Aim for the fill line (usually 8–10 mL per bottle).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- Not typically applicable — blood cultures are usually taken on inpatients or ED patients who are admitted. If a patient is discharged with pending cultures (e.g., low-risk cellulitis) — ensure they have a clear plan: **Return if fever worsens, rigors, spreading redness, or if unable to take oral antibiotics**.
- **Follow-up:** plan with GP or hospital microbiology call-back service.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:** If patient becomes haemodynamically unstable (MAP <65 mmHg despite fluid, lactate rising, NEWS2 ≥7) — cultures are being taken but priority is resuscitation.
- **Specific thresholds:** In sepsis: if lactate ≥4 mmol/L or hypotension not responding to 30 mL/kg fluid — activate sepsis protocol and call ICU/medical emergency team. Do not delay cultures but do not stop resuscitation to get them.

rawMarkdown:
## Blood Cultures — Taking
**One-liner:** Aseptic blood sampling (2-4 sets) from peripheral venipuncture before antibiotics in suspected sepsis — key to de-escalation and targeted therapy.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock with delayed antibiotics:** The single finding is hypotension (MAP <65 mmHg) + lactate ≥4 mmol/L. Cultures can wait 2–3 minutes for adequate preparation; start empiric antibiotics *immediately* after or even simultaneously. Do not delay antibiotics >60 minutes to obtain cultures.
- **Meningococcaemia with rapid purpura fulminans:** The finding is non-blanching petechiae/purpura that appear or spread within minutes to hours, plus fever. Cultures from two sites (blood and CSF if safe) are urgent but antibiotics should not be delayed for a lumbar puncture. Call senior and ICU early.

### Presentation
- Suspected infection: fever (>38°C), rigors, hypothermia (<36°C), tachycardia, tachypnoea, altered mental state, new hypotension.
- Localising signs may be absent (especially in elderly, immunocompromised).
- Indications: any patient with suspected bacteraemia including: sepsis, meningitis, infective endocarditis, pyrexia of unknown origin, central line infection, suspected osteomyelitis/septic arthritis, pneumonia requiring admission, and all patients with suspected serious infection before antibiotics.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Contaminant vs true pathogen:** The single best differentiator is *number of positive bottles* — ≥2 positive bottles (different sites) makes true pathogen likely; 1 of 2 sets positive (especially coagulase-negative staph) suggests contamination.
- **Bacteraemia vs viral or non-infectious fever:** Differentiate by procalcitonin (PCT) if available (PCT >0.5 ng/mL favours bacterial), but do not delay cultures waiting for it.
- **Endovascular infection (endocarditis, suppurative thrombophlebitis):** Look for persistent bacteraemia (≥2 sets positive over 12–24h), new murmur, embolic phenomena. Echocardiography is key.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Thorough hand hygiene, skin disinfection (2% chlorhexidine in 70% alcohol or alcohol-based povidone-iodine), allow to dry. Palpate vein *after* disinfection (not before). Collect aerobic + anaerobic bottles (or specific order per local protocol). Volume is key — draw 8–10 mL per bottle (less in children). Use sterile technique — do not touch the puncture site after disinfection. Change gloves if you accidentally contaminate.
- **Bloods:** Full blood count, CRP, PCT, lactate, coagulation profile, renal/liver function. Additional: consider cultures from other sites (urine, sputum, swabs, CSF if indicated).
- **Imaging:** Not directly part of cultures but guided by clinical focus (chest X-ray, CT abdomen, echocardiography).
- **Special tests:** If suspect infective endocarditis — obtain 3 sets of blood cultures (ideally over 24h) before antibiotics, from different venipunctures (not from line unless suspect line infection). For central line infection — paired cultures (peripheral + central) with differential time to positivity (central grows >2h earlier suggests line source).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** qSOFA (≥2 of: RR≥22, SBP≤100, altered mentation) or NEWS2 (≥5 triggers escalation). For endocarditis: Duke criteria (major criteria: 2 positive blood cultures typical organisms, echocardiographic vegetation).
- **Never-miss test:** **Ensure adequate volume** — most common error is underfilling bottles. Target 8–10 mL per bottle (adults). Also, **re-label if drawn from a line** — mark site clearly (e.g. "peripheral", "central line lumen A").

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - If not already done: insert IV/IO access (separate site for cultures).
    - Clean skin with 2% chlorhexidine in 70% alcohol swab — use a single-use applicator, allow 30 seconds to dry.
    - Do not repalpate after disinfection; use sterile gloves.
    - Obtaining cultures: fill aerobic bottle first (if using butterfly or straight draw) or follow local order. Invert gently after filling.
    - If blood is difficult: use smaller volumes rather than no culture — but note that yield drops significantly below 5 mL per bottle.
    - Document exact time and site of each culture in notes.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - Review culture results at 24–48h. If intermediate organisms appear (e.g. coagulase-negative staph) — discuss with microbiology to decide significance.
    - Ensure subcultures and sensitivities are requested if any growth.
    - Once organism identified, tailor antibiotics from empiric to targeted (de-escalate if possible).
    - Check for need for repeat cultures (e.g. persistent fever after 48h or to confirm clearance in endocarditis/line infections).
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Not applicable for the procedure itself; follow-up cultures as per infection focus.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A — procedure is single episode, not ongoing therapy. But if patient admitted with sepsis, set observation frequency (e.g. q1h for first 4 hours, then q2–4h), fluids as per local sepsis protocol (e.g. 30 mL/kg crystalloid in first 3h), VTE prophylaxis as per usual.

### Drugs & Doses
- No drugs for the procedure. But crucial: **Do not give antibiotics until after cultures are drawn unless delay would be unsafe** (septic shock). If given before, document time and ideally draw cultures within 10–15 minutes of the antibiotic (still useful).
- If central line suspected: consider empiric antibiotics per local guideline (e.g., vancomycin + antipseudomonal beta-lactam). Do not list doses here — [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Delaying antibiotics for >60 minutes while struggling to get cultures. **Get the cultures first, but only if it takes <5 minutes; otherwise skip and start antibiotics.** The error is prioritising the perfect culture over timely therapy.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - Contamination — leading to false-positive cultures (coagulase-negative staph, diphtheroids, Bacillus spp.) → unnecessary antibiotics, prolonged stay.
    - Needlestick injury to operator.
    - Haematoma, nerve injury (rare).
    - Iatrogenic anaemia (if frequent large-volume draws) — uncommon.
- **Chronic:** None direct from procedure.

### Prognosis
- Proper culture technique directly improves antibiotic stewardship — reduces mortality by enabling de-escalation. Contamination rates should be <3% (benchmark). Each false-positive culture can add 1–2 extra hospital days and costs.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO use a sterile technique checklist:** Hand hygiene, skin prep (chlorhexidine alcohol, allow to dry), sterile gloves, no repalpation, fill to mark, invert gently. Many hospitals have a bedside checklist — use it.
- **DO take two separate sets (from two different venipunctures) before antibiotics in all stable patients with suspected sepsis.** One set is insufficient — it may miss intermittent bacteraemia.
- **DO document the site, time, and any technical difficulties** — this helps microbiology interpret results (e.g., if only 1 set positive and it was a difficult draw from a small vein, contamination more likely).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **DO NOT use alcohol swabs that have dried out** — they have reduced efficacy. Use a new one.
- **DO NOT take blood cultures from a line** unless you suspect line infection (and then do paired sets per protocol). If you do, label it clearly. Routinely using line draws increases contamination.
- **DO NOT underfill or overfill bottles** — underfill reduces yield, overfill may hemolyse or activate anticoagulant. Aim for the fill line (usually 8–10 mL per bottle).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- Not typically applicable — blood cultures are usually taken on inpatients or ED patients who are admitted. If a patient is discharged with pending cultures (e.g., low-risk cellulitis) — ensure they have a clear plan: **Return if fever worsens, rigors, spreading redness, or if unable to take oral antibiotics**.
- **Follow-up:** plan with GP or hospital microbiology call-back service.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:** If patient becomes haemodynamically unstable (MAP <65 mmHg despite fluid, lactate rising, NEWS2 ≥7) — cultures are being taken but priority is resuscitation.
- **Specific thresholds:** In sepsis: if lactate ≥4 mmol/L or hypotension not responding to 30 mL/kg fluid — activate sepsis protocol and call ICU/medical emergency team. Do not delay cultures but do not stop resuscitation to get them.
